plan.goal: The first 1,000 words in ~15 lessons — audio-first, recycled relentlessly, Modern pronunciation with vocabulary chosen for Koine/Classical continuity.

roadmap.focus: 25 core nouns + 25 core verbs, maximally continuous. Straight into declension & tense through short sentences.
The function-word skeleton: articles fully, pronouns, the commonest prepositions, and/but/if/because, question words, numbers 1–10. + more everyday nouns/verbs.
Family, body, food, house, time-of-day, weather; verbs of motion, speech, perception. First adjectives (good/big/small).
City & travel, work & money, nature & animals; more tense (future, past habitual), the genitive, possessives in full.
Abstract high-frequency nouns (time, way, thing, reason, part), the 100 commonest adjectives, comparatives, adverbs.
The long tail of the top-1000: connectives, discourse words, abstractions, and the rest of the frequency list. By here, micro-stories run entirely on known words.

principles: Frequency first, continuity-biased, and words chosen so they INTERACT — the sentence bank stays simple because the words naturally combine.
Every lesson recycles all prior words; a word met in L01 should be heard 25+ times by L05.
Grammar is delivered through sentences and an English teacher's explanation, not paradigm drills — the paradigms live in the Grammar tab for reference.

lessons.title: Fifty words that already make sentences
lessons.blurb: Twenty-five nouns and twenty-five verbs — almost all continuous from ancient to modern Greek. Then a long series of very short sentences where they interact naturally, with an English teacher explaining exactly enough grammar to make each one correct.

words.cont: Unchanged from antiquity. Gives anthropo- (anthropology, philanthropy).
Ancient ἀνήρ, stem ἀνδρ- → andro-, android, Andrew. Modern άντρας/άνδρας.
Ancient γυνή, stem γυναικ- → gyneco-, misogyny. Recognizable as γυναίκα.
From παιδίον; παῖς/παιδ- → ped-, pedagogy, encyclopedia.
μήτηρ → μητέρα; cf. Latin mater, English maternal, metropolis (mother-city).
πατήρ → πατέρας; pater, paternal, patriarch.
Unchanged; philo- (philosophy, Philadelphia).
Unchanged; theo- (theology, atheist, enthusiasm).
DRIFT: ancient bread = ἄρτος (still the liturgical word). Everyday modern ψωμί, from ψωμίον 'morsel'.
DRIFT: Classical ὕδωρ (hydro-, hydrant). Modern νερό, from νηρόν 'fresh (water)'.
DRIFT: ancient οἶκος (eco-, economy, ecology). Modern σπίτι, from Latin hospitium.
βιβλίον → βιβλίο (final -ν dropped); Bible, bibliography.
πόλις → πόλη; polis, metropolis, politics, police.
Unchanged — the cry of Xenophon's ten thousand: θάλασσα!
Unchanged; helio-, heliocentric, perihelion.
ἡμέρα → μέρα/ημέρα; ephemeral, ephemeris.
Ancient νύξ, stem νυκτ- → νύχτα; nyctalopia, cf. Latin nox.
Modern δρόμος 'road, course' → -drome (hippodrome, aerodrome). Ancient street = ὁδός → method, -ode.
χείρ → χέρι; chiro- (chiropractor), surgery (χειρουργία 'hand-work').
DRIFT: ancient ὀφθαλμός (ophthalmology). Modern μάτι, from ὀμμάτιον 'little eye'.
καρδία → καρδιά; cardio-, cardiac, cordial (Latin cognate).
Unchanged; phone-, telephone, symphony, phonetics.
Unchanged; logos, logic, -logy, dialogue. (John 1:1.)
Unchanged; zoo-, zodiac, protozoa.
Unchanged; onomatopoeia, synonym, -onym, cf. Latin nomen.
From εἰμί; the 3rd-person είναι descends from ἐστί (John 1:1 ἦν is its imperfect).
Unchanged ἔχω 'I have, hold'.
Ancient θέλω/ἐθέλω 'I wish'; root of monothelitism.
βλέπω; irregular past είδε 'saw' (same root as 'idea', 'video').
ἀκούω; acoustic, acoustics.
λέγω → λέω; the -logos/-logy root, dialect, lexicon.
Modern τρώω from τρώγω; irregular past έφαγε 'ate' (root ἔφαγον → -phagous, esophagus).
πίνω; irregular past ήπιε 'drank'. Cf. Latin cognate → potion, potable.
γράφω; graph-, -graphy, grammar; past έγραψε.
Modern διαβάζω (ancient 'read aloud' was ἀναγιγνώσκω); past διάβασε.
Modern ξέρω; ancient γιγνώσκω → gnostic, diagnosis; οἶδα.
ἀγαπάω → αγαπώ; the noun ἀγάπη, agape.
δίδωμι → δίνω; dose, antidote, anecdote.
φέρω → φέρνω; -phore (metaphor, amphora), cf. Latin ferre.
ἔρχομαι — a deponent (middle form, active sense), then and now.
Modern πηγαίνω/πάω; ancient 'go' = βαίνω → -basis, base, acrobat.
μένω 'remain'; cf. Latin manere, English remain, permanent.
ζάω/ζῶ → ζω; zoo-, biology, the noun ζωή.
εὑρίσκω → βρίσκω; Archimedes' εὕρηκα — 'I have found it!'
φοβέομαι → φοβάμαι (deponent); phobia, hydrophobia.
τρέχω; trochee, trochanter (a 'runner').
περιπατέω → περπατώ; peripatetic (Aristotle taught while walking).
κοιμάομαι (deponent); cemetery = κοιμητήριον, 'sleeping-place'.
ἀνοίγω; unchanged.
φεύγω; cf. Latin fugere → fugitive, refuge.

sentences.explain: Ο άνθρωπος — 'the person'. 'Ο' is the masculine word for 'the', and the subject of a sentence stands in the nominative — its plain, dictionary form. είναι — 'is'. εδώ — 'here'.

Ο άντρας — 'the man', the subject, nominative. τρώει — 'eats', third person singular, present tense. ψωμί — 'bread', the object. Objects take the accusative — but for neuter nouns like ψωμί, the accusative looks identical to the nominative.
A feminine noun now: η γυναίκα. 'Η' is the feminine 'the'. πίνει — 'drinks'. νερό — 'water', neuter, the object.
Το παιδί — 'the child'. 'Το' is the neuter 'the'. διαβάζει — 'reads'. ένα βιβλίο — 'a book'; 'ένα' is the neuter 'a' or 'one', used only when you mean one particular thing.

βλέπει — 'sees'. τη θάλασσα — 'the sea'. Watch the article: as a subject the feminine 'the' is η, but as an OBJECT it becomes την — and before certain sounds the -ν drops, giving τη. Your first look at the article declining.
η μητέρα — 'the mother'. αγαπάει — 'loves', from αγαπώ, the root of the Greek word agápe. το παιδί — 'the child', object; neuter, so unchanged.

sentences.gr: Ο φίλος μου έρχεται στο σπίτι.
sentences.en: My friend comes to the house.
sentences.explain: ο φίλος μου — 'my friend'; 'μου' placed after the noun means 'my'. έρχεται — 'comes', a deponent verb: middle in form, active in meaning, exactly as in ancient Greek. στο σπίτι — 'to the house'; the preposition σε plus το merges into στο.

sentences.gr: Χθες το παιδί διάβασε ένα βιβλίο.
sentences.en: Yesterday the child read a book.
sentences.explain: Χθες — 'yesterday'. Now the past: διάβασε — 'read', the simple past (aorist) of διαβάζω. Notice the ending becomes -ε for he/she/it in the past.

έφαγε — 'ate'. The past of τρώω is completely irregular, from the ancient root ἔφαγον — just like English 'eat / ate'. Greek's commonest verbs have irregular pasts; learn them as pairs: τρώει / έφαγε.
ήπιε — 'drank', the irregular past of πίνω. Present πίνει, past ήπιε.
είδε — 'saw', the irregular past of βλέπω. Present βλέπει, past είδε — the same ancient root that gives us 'idea' and, through Latin, 'video'.
δεν — 'not'. To negate a verb, put δεν directly in front of it: δεν τρώει, 'does not eat'. This δεν descends from the ancient οὐδέν, 'nothing'.
βλέπω — 'I see', the first-person form. τον ήλιο — 'the sun'. ήλιος is masculine, and as the object it takes the accusative: ο ήλιος becomes τον ήλιο — both the article and the noun change.

sentences.gr: Ο φίλος μου έχει ένα σπίτι.
sentences.explain: έχει — 'has', from έχω. ένα σπίτι — 'a house', neuter.
και — 'and', unchanged for three thousand years. Two feminine nouns, both nominative: η ημέρα, η νύχτα.
The plural. Το παιδί, 'the child', becomes τα παιδιά, 'the children' — the neuter plural 'the' is τα and the noun ends in -ά. τρέχουν — 'they run', with the plural ending -ουν.

sentences.gr: Ο άντρας και η γυναίκα περπατούν στον δρόμο.
sentences.en: The man and the woman walk in the street.
sentences.explain: Two subjects joined by και take a plural verb: περπατούν, 'they walk'. στον δρόμο — 'in the street': σε plus τον, the masculine accusative.
Πού — 'where'. And the punctuation: a Greek question ends with what looks like a semicolon ( ; ), never a question mark.
κοιμάται — 'sleeps, is sleeping', from κοιμάμαι, another deponent verb. Its root gives us 'cemetery' — literally a sleeping-place.
ζει — 'lives', from ζω — the zoo- of zoology, and the noun ζωή, 'life'. One of the shortest and oldest words in the language.
